VP_1.1: The imperishable reality of language, beginningless and endless like Brahman, unfolds as the world-process by appearing in the form of meaning.
VP_1.2: What is taught as one appears as though divided because it rests upon different powers; distinction emerges without real separateness.
VP_1.3: Birth, change, growth, decline, and the other transformations arise when time is superimposed on what is in itself whole.
VP_1.4: The single source of everything appears in many modes at once: as enjoyer, enjoyed world, and the very process of experience.
VP_1.5: The Veda is both the means of reaching that reality and an echo of it; though one, it is taught along many distinct paths.
VP_1.6: The many branches of tradition display plurality in method while still converging in action; language works according to the power available in each form.
VP_1.7: The many scriptural traditions, aimed at seen and unseen ends alike, still depend on that one ground and are inferred from its signs by the knowers of Veda.
VP_1.8: Doctrines of monism and dualism, along with many other debates, arise from interpretive constructions that lean on that same foundational speech.
VP_1.9: True purification there is said to be knowledge itself, arriving by a single path and aligned with the syllable Om that does not conflict with the many schools.
VP_1.10: The diverse disciplines are like limbs and sub-limbs of the world's ordering principle; their differences train and refine understanding.
VP_1.11: Grammar is said by the wise to be the nearest austerity to Brahman, the highest discipline among disciplines, and the first limb of the Veda.
VP_1.12: There is a supreme savor of speech once its forms are properly distinguished, and this path leads toward that most luminous and sacred light.
VP_1.13: Words alone ground the real principles by which meanings function, and without grammar there is no true understanding of words.
VP_1.14: This is the gateway to release, the medicine for the impurities of speech, and the purifying over-discipline that illuminates all other disciplines.
VP_1.15: Just as all classes of things are grounded in verbal form, so too this science is the final resort of the sciences in the world.
VP_1.24: Some words explain, while others are themselves the bearers of meaning; language always works through layered roles.
VP_1.46: Some old authorities say there is a real distinction between the self and its expressions; others say the apparent difference comes only from differences in cognition.
VP_1.122: What appears divided in experience is, at root, a manifestation of a deeper flash of understanding.
VP_2.1: The sentence is fundamentally one and partless; sequence is something the mind gathers and composes.
VP_2.2: Philosophers differ widely: some treat the word as primary, some the dependent part, and some the sentence as the real unit of meaning.
VP_2.3: Grammar draws distinctions such as pause and expectancy for analytical purposes; not every part of speech behaves according to one uniform rule.
VP_2.4: A sentence may be defined through mutually expectant parts, through a word that governs the others, or through a single integrated meaning shaped by qualifiers.
VP_2.5: Even a vocative can function by specifying an action, as in calling out 'Come, Devadatta'; sentence force depends on how the parts work together.
VP_2.6: Just as several absolutives can qualify one finite verb, so too a finite verb may, in analysis, be taken as qualifying another within a larger unity.
VP_2.7: Just as one light is mentally divided in keeping with different visible forms, one sentence-meaning too is grasped through seeming divisions.
VP_2.8: Just as one image can be described through many distinct features like blue or bright, one linguistic whole can be approached through different analytic distinctions.
VP_2.9: In the same way, one complete sentence, lacking any further need, may still be retold by means of other dependent words.
VP_2.10: Just as roots, suffixes, and the like are analytically extracted from a word, so words too can be extracted from the sentence for explanatory purposes.
VP_2.11: Similarity across letters appears even within parts of letters, just as parts of words can appear to resemble other full words.
VP_2.12: Expressions like 'bring the bull', 'bring water', or 'bring barley' include parts that are meaningless by themselves; practical use depends on their pattern of combination and exclusion.
VP_2.13: If speech itself has no real division, how could meaning be truly divided? The untrained mind takes procedural distinctions to be differences in reality.
VP_2.14: Just as there is no separate 'brahminness' in a brahmin's blanket, names like Devadatta can become empty if they are detached from the sentence that gives them function.
VP_2.15: Once the general meaning is obscured, it does not remain standing separately in the particular; if what was taken up is abandoned, where exactly does that abandoned content remain?
VP_2.16: If sentence-meaning were non-verbal, then word-meaning would have to be non-verbal too, and the very bond between word and meaning would collapse.
VP_2.17: For some thinkers, so-called specific words are only images of a more general meaning, and hearers bring out their force through connection with other words.
VP_2.18: For them, the whole sentence-meaning is completed through these differentiated manifestations, and meaning becomes established in hearers through explicit and suggestive expression together.
VP_2.19: The spoken word that is recited softly appears manifest and sequential, but the cognition in which it finally settles is spread out and non-sequential.
VP_2.20: Just as no difference of action is grasped in each individual act of raising, while repetition reveals a class through motions such as turning, so too a pattern emerges across instances.
VP_2.21: In letters, words, and sentences alike, hearing presents similar manifestations; though the underlying reality is utterly distinct, it can still appear as if of one form.
VP_2.22: If these are eternal, how can there be a real before and after among them? It is one and the same power that appears in this sequential way.
VP_2.23: Just as 'long' and 'quick' are known without positing different times, so the distinction of short and long too shines forth through a single underlying feature.
VP_2.24: What is eternal cannot really be divided merely by sequence; and if the differentiating units are absent, what sort of sequence could remain at all?
VP_2.39: A person grasps meaning somehow through sentence-bound distinctions, whether those distinctions are taken as fused, separated, or differently classified.
VP_2.118: Some take habitual usage itself to be the convention that points from one thing to the next, indicating that 'this follows from that.'
VP_2.120: The form grasped through repeated usage and observed practice is not itself the word; it depends on another effort of cognition.
VP_2.144: That inner operation is established within each knower, yet even the one acting through it cannot fully describe it from the outside.
VP_2.489: Discernment grows by engaging many scriptural traditions; one can only go so far by chasing personal reasoning alone.
VP_3.1.1: Some divide the word into two, four, or five parts by analytically extracting pieces from the sentence, much as one isolates roots and suffixes.
VP_3.1.2: When meaning is analytically extracted, thinkers describe universals or substances as the lasting referents underlying all words.
VP_3.1.3: For some, universality is indicated through associative force; when the intended bearer lacks capacity, another capable instance stands in its place.
VP_3.1.4: Dependence leaves a remainder that must be learned by rule, as with means of knowledge; so even where a universal is named, what lacks operative force is not really grasped.
VP_3.1.5: If mere conjunction were all that mattered, binding would stop there; but once intention is involved, grammatical markers arise from the locus of semantic power.
VP_3.1.6: Words first point toward their own universal form, and then the mind extends that pattern into the universals it attributes to things.
VP_3.1.7: Just as redness can be recognized in dye and then in a cloth through association, so a universal can be apprehended through what carries it.
VP_3.1.8: Because of the bond between word and meaning, a universal is also established in the word itself and becomes available when meaning-universals are named.
VP_3.1.9: When only the term 'universal' remains, it is treated as the universal of universals; likewise, the class of word-universals is spoken of through a higher-order class.
VP_3.1.10: The universal present in words about word-universals is distinct from the words themselves, yet it continues without abandoning its own status as a word-universal.
VP_3.1.24: Just as the senses are apprehended as a coordinated whole within the self, related structures too can be grasped as wholes within relation itself.
VP_3.1.110: Knowledge is not grasped in the same way as an external object, and a thing's own nature cannot be fixed once abstracted entirely from what it appears as.
VP_3.2.1: What is called substance has its recognized equivalents, and it is remembered as something enduring beneath changing expression.
VP_3.2.2: A real thing is determined by unreal appearances, and true reference is often made through words that rely on provisional attributes.
VP_3.2.3: Just as one says 'Devadatta's house' by means of a shifting circumstance, the word still points to the house itself in its cleaner sense.
VP_3.2.4: Gold may be joined to many temporary forms like bracelets and ornaments, yet the underlying substance still remains what the word finally reaches.
VP_3.2.5: Specific forms restrict an otherwise wide range of reference, much as tubes and channels limit the broad capacity of the senses.
VP_3.2.6: When a word operates through such forms, it still speaks of what is enduring because that patterned appearance too rests in the thing's reality.
VP_3.2.7: The elders teach that there is no final difference between what is real and what is unreal; what gets dismissed as unreal is often only reality left unexamined.
VP_3.2.8: The undivided real takes on the form of conceptual distinction, and though no real temporal difference exists there, a temporal difference is nevertheless grasped.
VP_3.2.9: Just as the properties of an external object cannot literally reside in cognition, cognition can still be established as if identical with it while remaining utterly different in nature.
VP_3.2.10: Forms taken as mere modifications cannot fully account for what a thing is in itself; identity and non-identity become unstable when pushed too far.
VP_3.3.1: The relation among meanings is held in place through uttered words.
VP_3.3.2: Doubt can arise about the object or even about the cognition of it, but once the relevant forms are directly grasped, deviation has no place.
VP_3.3.3: The genitive relation lets us say 'this word denotes that meaning'; from this, the very reality of the word-meaning bond is spoken of as a relation.
VP_3.3.4: No separate word directly denotes relation in its own nature; because relation is radically dependent, it does not present an independent form of its own.
VP_3.3.5: Wherever a supporting function is found, relation is inferred there: it is the power behind powers and the qualifying feature behind features.
VP_3.3.6: Through attributes that belong to them, connection and inherence are spoken of indirectly, and even those rely on fixed limiting conditions in order to assist anything at all.
VP_3.3.7: A given state alone, marked by the production of an effect, is the condition in which one thing comes into conjunction with another.
VP_3.3.8: Where non-substantial entities arise, the regularity found there alone is what accounts for their non-separability when the relevant access conditions do not differ.
VP_3.3.24: When determination itself becomes the object of a determinate act of knowing, cognition stands forth in its own proper mode.
VP_3.5.1: Whatever is apprehended as differentiating something while remaining dependent on it is what the tradition calls a quality.
VP_3.6.1: Direction, instrumentality, action, and time are named as things, yet as powers of meaning they are never fixed in one absolute form.
VP_3.7.1: In the accomplishment of action, effective capacity is what the learned call the means.
VP_3.7.2: Because the world is made of many powers and properties, what is intended in a given case is selected according to context and purpose.
VP_3.7.3: Our talk of means depends on how understanding is arranged; among forms that are or are not materially distinct, difference is often projected by cognition.
VP_3.7.4: When the mind first gathers Panchalas and Kurus into one intended whole and the speaker later separates them again, the sense of distinction newly appears.
VP_3.7.5: Forms shaped by words and taken up by the mind can be treated almost like perceptible objects, such as Kamsa and the like, when they are assigned the role of means.
VP_3.7.6: Speakers project the movement of understanding onto things and, on that basis, construct different powers and functions within the objects spoken of.
VP_3.7.7: When an action generated from word and object becomes manifest, its status as a means is likewise established as something projected in cognitive form.
VP_3.7.8: Independence, dependence, and sequence are all shown to rest on conceptual construction, even in entities that are themselves inert.
VP_3.7.9: For theorists of relation, powers and their bearers are all real entities, and among them instrumentality is explained even where no direct word names it.
VP_3.7.10: When a pot is the object of sight, largeness and similar features function as means; when color is the object of sight, colorhood and the like function as means.
VP_3.7.167: What functions as an instrument cannot simply collapse into the action itself; otherwise the distinct reality of the acting thing would dissolve.
